payload.report_md:
# US Stock Watcher — Next-Session Forecast for Monday, July 13

**Bullish lean: Strong expansion / bull · composite score +37 · confidence 48%**

*The next U.S. regular session is Monday, July 13, opening at 9:30 AM ET (22:30 KST). Data as of 2026-07-11T22:09:00; data quality: fresh.*

## Executive Summary

Heading into Monday's open, the composite market-state score stands at +37 out of 100, placing the setup firmly in "Strong expansion / bull" territory, though confidence in that read is moderate at 48% given that only four components of the model are currently measured. Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning remain unmeasured and have been reweighted out of the composite for this brief, so the bullish lean should be treated as directionally informative rather than fully corroborated.

## Market Diagnosis

**Indices.** Friday's close leaves the major benchmarks set up as follows heading into Monday: the S&P 500 finished at 7,575.39 (+0.42% on the day), the Nasdaq-100 at 29,825.11 (+0.33%), and the Dow at 52,637.01 (+0.29%). The Russell 2000 was the outlier, slipping 0.49% to close at 2,977.81 — a divergence worth watching as small caps lagged the broader advance.

**Market drivers.** Three of four tracked drivers are currently supportive of the bullish case: volatility, index trend, and the yield curve. The lone headwind is the U.S. dollar, which continues to work against the risk-on narrative.

**Breadth & concentration.** The overall market-state score of +37/100 reinforces the "Strong expansion / bull" classification at 48% confidence across the four measured components. As noted above, six components — Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning — remain unmeasured and were reweighted out of this calculation.

**Sector & rotation.** Leadership into the new session is concentrated in Financials, Health Care, and Industrials, while Consumer Staples, Real Estate, and Energy are lagging — a rotation pattern consistent with a risk-on, cyclically-tilted tape rather than a defensive posture.

**Rates, dollar & commodities.** The 10-year Treasury yield sits at 4.54%, and the dollar index (DXY) is at 100.97, up a modest 0.03% on the day. In commodities, WTI crude fell 0.93% to 71.41, and gold declined 0.64% to 4,104.10 — both pulling back even as equities and the dollar held firm.

## Outlook

**Short term.** Framed specifically for Monday's session, the near-term bias is constructive, consistent with the "Strong expansion / bull" market-state reading at 48% confidence. This lean would flip should the overall composite score cross back below zero.

**Medium term.** The medium-term view carries the same constructive bias tied to the "Strong expansion / bull" state and 48% confidence level, with the same conditional trigger: a break of the composite score below zero would invalidate the bullish framing.

**Medium-to-long term.** Extending further out, the medium-to-long-term outlook likewise leans constructive under the current "Strong expansion / bull" regime and 48% confidence, again subject to reversal if the overall score moves negative.

## Top News

Coverage feeding into Monday's setup includes a look at an overlooked, cheaper alternative to the widely-held VOO S&P 500 ETF (AOL.com); Investor's Business Daily's read on whether the market is ready to extend gains, with Nvidia, Micron, and Sandisk approaching buy points ahead of major earnings; a Globe and Mail piece questioning why Bitcoin remains below $65,000 even as oil trades below $76; a Stock Titan report on JPMorgan (NYSE: JPM) selling $775,000 in buffered notes linked to S&P 500 futures; a TradingKey analysis probing why Nvidia, up just 7% year-to-date, is significantly underperforming the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index; and a Motley Fool piece arguing Coca-Cola is outperforming both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100, with a fresh case for buying the stock in July.

## What Changed Since Yesterday

No prior brief — this is the first publication.
